title: PROP-041 amendment-002 — add Category F (editorial section-split, propose-only), Category G (internal forward-reference preview), and Category H (outbound link-out preview, narrowed scope) to the sloppytoppy-rewrite taxonomy
operator_decisions_applied: D-A approve Category F (editorial section-split). D-B approve Category G (forward-reference preview). D-C content-dense policy: WIN surfaces are NEVER content-dense eligible; long WIN debunks should link to underlying research rather than expand inline. D-D onboarding batch_cap 5 → 3 during curmudgeon-calibration period (already applied in commit 4cbcecb; mentioned here for completeness; not re-patched in this amendment). 2026-05-16 EXP-401 compression-experiment addendum: Category H narrowed to 'mechanical link-preview move' only; real content compression (3-5x reduction with editorial judgment) goes through analyst-commissioned mode, not the rewriter.
scope_disclaimer: DESIGN AMENDMENT ONLY. Tinker authors this single comprehensive amendment for operator review. Tinker does NOT self-apply any patch per directive out_of_scope_for_tinker[0]. After operator approval, operator + cowork-claude apply the patches in a coordinated session. No new categories beyond F/G/H are proposed here per directive out_of_scope_for_tinker[1].
relationship_to_parent: Sibling additive document to PROP-041 and PROP-041-amendment-001-implementation. The parent design enumerates categories A/B/C/D/E; this amendment extends the enum to A/B/C/D/E/F/G/H and adds the per-category mechanical instructions, RWR rubric entries, schema field extensions, and audit-script behavior. PROP-041 status remains 'design-pending-operator-review' until operator promotes; this amendment lands as 'implementation-pending-operator-review' awaiting operator approval before patches apply.

category_F_editorial_section_split.trigger: section_details_block surface where length is the dominant defect (target_max exceeded by >2.5x) AND Categories A/B/C/D/E cannot bring composite above acceptable_floor
category_F_editorial_section_split.rewriter_move: PROPOSE-ONLY split-plan. Rewriter does NOT modify sections.json. Rewriter writes an RW-NNN.json record with a new proposed_block_boundaries[] field listing 4-6 sub-section title-hints plus excerpt-snippets identifying natural argument boundaries. Operator + cowork-claude review and apply the split via the standard sections.json edit path.
category_F_editorial_section_split.win_eligibility: NOT WIN-eligible. Section_details_block surfaces only. Per addendum + operator policy D-C, long WIN surfaces route to analyst-commissioned compression mode, not Category F.
category_F_editorial_section_split.stakes: Highest of F/G/H. Changes the surface set itself (one surface becomes 4-6). Operator-applied editorial decision.

category_G_forward_reference_preview.trigger: forward_references subtraction firing on a surface where the rewriter can resolve the referenced content from its own clone (cross-references to other sections, WINs, parts, predictions)
category_G_forward_reference_preview.rewriter_move: Read the referenced surface's <summary>/claim_tldr/section title and paraphrase into a 1-sentence preview that explains why the reader should follow the reference. Replace 'See Section 1.8' with 'Section 1.8 closes the refraction-fix escape — H(r) cannot be reinterpreted as apparent without invalidating the Polaris calibration.'
category_G_forward_reference_preview.rwr_5_carveout: Paraphrase of existing content (the referenced surface's summary/TLDR) is NOT a new claim. Re-framing of existing content into a new framing IS a new claim and rejected by curmudgeon RWR-5.
category_G_forward_reference_preview.stakes: Lowest of F/G/H. Mechanical, judgment-bound, scoped to existing content.

category_H_outbound_link_preview.trigger: outbound link (<a href="...">) or 'see [URL]' / 'see [page]' reference where preview content cannot be auto-read from the clone (external URLs, longer-form analysis pages outside the rewriter's read-scope, citations to external papers/datasets)
category_H_outbound_link_preview.rewriter_move: Read the link anchor text + surrounding 1-2 sentences. Synthesize a 1-sentence preview that explains what the reader will find at the link. NARROW SCOPE per addendum: this is a mechanical link-preview move, NOT a depth-content-replacement move. Rewriter does NOT relocate paragraphs to other pages.
category_H_outbound_link_preview.rwr_5_carveout: Preview must be a paraphrase of context already present in the surface — anchor text plus adjacent prose. Synthesizing a new claim about external content (claiming the linked paper proves X when the surface doesn't already say so) is a new claim and rejected.
category_H_outbound_link_preview.d_c_alignment: H is NOT the operational mechanism for D-C. D-C's operational mechanism is analyst-commissioned content compression mode (HNOTE → analyst → EXP). H stays as a strictly mechanical link-preview move.

scope_summary.categories_summary_one_liner: F = propose section split (architectural, operator-applied). G = preview internal cross-ref (mechanical, auto-readable source). H = preview outbound link (mechanical, anchor-text-only source). All three propose-only; all three audit-script + curmudgeon RWR validated.

phasing_recommendation.rationale: Per operator preference: F is architecturally invasive (interacts with sections.json schema, scores.json record-keeping, cooldown, content_hash). G and H are mechanical extensions of the existing rewriter loop that add no new file-ownership questions. Splitting the amendment into two phased deliveries minimizes risk and gives the operator a clean rollback boundary if F's architecture audit reveals an unforeseen issue.
phasing_recommendation.phase_2_immediate: G + H + the supporting schema/rubric/audit-script changes that are independent of F. These can land without changing the surface set or sections.json. The rewriter gains two new category tools that address ~25% of the eligible queue's defect profile (forward_references is the second-largest understandability subtraction across below-floor surfaces per first-run analysis).
phasing_recommendation.phase_2_1_deferred: F. Lands after operator architecture-audit of the sections.json interaction model. If the audit reveals an irreconcilable issue, F is dropped and the alternative (analyst-commissioned content-architecture refactor) is recorded as the long-term path for oversized section blocks.
phasing_recommendation.alternative_under_consideration: Single-phase landing of F+G+H together if operator's architecture audit returns clean. This amendment's patches are written so either path is viable; the phasing is a operational choice, not a structural one.

F_Q1_propose_vs_apply.question: Does F propose new <details> blocks (modifying sections.json + HTML generation), or only flag for operator/analyst attention with a proposed split-plan?
F_Q1_propose_vs_apply.answer: PROPOSE-ONLY. F surfaces a structured split-plan in the RW record. The rewriter does NOT write to sections.json. Operator + cowork-claude review the proposed boundaries and apply the split via the standard sections.json edit path. This mirrors the existing PROP-041 propose-only discipline (rewriter never writes to wins.json/sections.json) and avoids the schema-mutation question entirely at Phase 2 launch.
F_Q1_propose_vs_apply.rationale: Three reasons. (1) Auto-applied splits make a multi-thousand-word block disappear from sections.json in a single commit — un-reviewable diff. (2) Natural argument boundaries are judgment calls that benefit from operator's editorial taste. (3) The rewriter's confidence in proposed boundaries can be expressed via the recommendation strength field; operator decides whether to apply, adjust, or defer.

F_Q2_RW_output_shape.question: If F proposes new blocks, what's the RW output shape?
F_Q2_RW_output_shape.answer: Single RW-NNN.json with a new top-level field proposed_block_boundaries[]. The array has 4-6 entries; each entry has: split_index (integer 0..N), proposed_subblock_id (string, suggested <details id='X'> attribute), proposed_subblock_title (string, the new <summary> text), starts_at_excerpt (string, ~30 words from the original_text marking the boundary), rationale (1-sentence explanation of why this is a natural argument break), independent_scoreable (boolean: rewriter's prediction whether the sub-block will independently lift above acceptable_floor after split). The original_text field carries the full pre-split content; the rewritten_text field is NULL (no inline rewrite — F is a structural proposal, not a content rewrite).
F_Q2_RW_output_shape.rationale_for_single_file: A multi-file RW (one parent + N children) multiplies the curmudgeon-on-rewrite review burden and complicates rewrite-attempts.json bookkeeping. Single-file F keeps the existing 1-RW-per-surface model. Sub-block-level rewrites (mechanical Category A/B/C/D/E passes) happen AFTER the split lands and sloppytoppy-score re-scores the new sub-blocks; those are separate RWs in subsequent runs.

F_Q3_curmudgeon_validation.question: How does curmudgeon validate an F rewrite?
F_Q3_curmudgeon_validation.answer: Two new RWR entries (RWR-10, RWR-11) added to monitor/prompts/reference/sloppytoppy-rewrite-rubric.md. RWR-10 (natural argument boundaries): for each proposed boundary, curmudgeon confirms the starts_at_excerpt sits at a topic shift, logical-pivot, or argument-completion point — NOT mid-sentence, NOT mid-claim. Major fail if a boundary slices a load-bearing logical structure (e.g., proposes a split between an IF and its THEN). RWR-11 (no content loss/reorder + independent-scoreable plausibility): curmudgeon walks the proposed sub-blocks in order and confirms (a) every paragraph from original_text appears in exactly one sub-block, (b) sub-block order matches original_text order (no reordering), (c) the rewriter's independent_scoreable prediction is defensible for each sub-block.
F_Q3_curmudgeon_validation.decision_thresholds: Same as RWR-1..9: 0 major + 0 moderate + 0-2 minor → approve. 1+ major OR 2+ moderate OR 3+ minor → reject. RWR-10 is the highest-stakes new check (a slice through a load-bearing IF/THEN breaks the argument).

F_Q4_interaction_with_scores_cooldown_co.question: F changes the surface set itself. How does this interact with content_hash, scores.json record-keeping, and cooldown_days?
F_Q4_interaction_with_scores_cooldown_co.answer: Three-step lifecycle. (1) F-RW lands as status='approved' after curmudgeon RWR-10/11 pass. (2) Decider Step 1m.B detects status='approved' AND surface_type='section_details_block' AND proposed_block_boundaries is non-empty → does NOT auto-integrate. Instead, sets RW.status='editorial-split-pending-operator' (new status added to the state machine) and writes an HNOTE-OPERATOR-F-PROPOSAL-<NNN> with the proposed boundaries flagged for operator attention. (3) Operator + cowork-claude reviews, applies the split via sections.json edit (creating 4-6 new <details> blocks), commits. The original surface_id (e.g., 'part4.html#p4-41-0') disappears from sections.json. New surface_ids (e.g., 'part4.html#p4-41-0a' through 'part4.html#p4-41-0e') are introduced. On the next sloppytoppy-score run (daily 03:30 UTC), the scorer's surface-enumeration step picks up the new IDs and discards the old (no-longer-existing) ID. (4) Operator manually marks the F-RW as status='integrated' with integration_commit set to the operator's editorial-split commit. The decider DOES NOT do this — it would require detecting cross-commit content moves, which is beyond Step 1m.B's read scope. **Content_hash:** the original surface's content_hash is referenced one last time during F-RW intake (Step 1m.A step 2 freshness check); after the split lands, the new sub-blocks have fresh content_hashes computed by sloppytoppy-score's normal flow. **Cooldown_days:** F-RW uses an elevated cooldown_days = 30 (vs default 14) — gives the operator time to review and apply the editorial split before the rewriter tries again. **scores.json record-keeping:** the old surface's score record stays in scores.json (sloppytoppy-score uses an additive write pattern), but the surface is dropped from the eligible queue once it disappears from wins.json/sections.json — the next sloppytoppy-score run will detect this and mark the record with a status='surface-no-longer-exists' flag (new field added to score record schema; small downstream change).
F_Q4_interaction_with_scores_cooldown_co.schema_change_to_score_record: Add optional field 'status' to score record (default null = active). Values: null (active), 'surface-no-longer-exists' (set by sloppytoppy-score when its surface-enumeration step fails to find the surface_id in current wins.json/sections.json). This is a small additive change; existing scorer code reads/writes the record unaffected. Documented in DATA-SCHEMAS.md alongside the RW schema additions.

F_Q5_WIN_eligibility.question: Is F WIN-eligible (could a 2000-word WIN.detail_evidence be split into multiple WIN surfaces?), or section-block only?
F_Q5_WIN_eligibility.answer: NOT WIN-eligible. F applies to section_details_block surfaces ONLY. Per operator policy D-C (content-dense flag policy: WIN surfaces are NEVER content-dense eligible) and per the 2026-05-16 EXP-401 compression-experiment finding, long WIN surfaces have two correct remediation paths: (1) analyst-commissioned content compression mode (HNOTE → analyst → EXP, the EXP-401 model where the 1888w WIN-012.detail_evidence was compressed to 528w with operator confirming 'much better'); (2) for the rare case where the WIN actually contains structurally-separate sub-arguments that belong as their own WINs, analyst Mode 2b (issue-driven re-examination of existing WINs) is the right path — not Phase 2 rewriter automation.
F_Q5_WIN_eligibility.rationale_for_exclusion: Three reasons. (1) WIN-level structural changes (creating new WIN-NNN entries from a sub-argument of an existing WIN) affect counts the dashboard displays, EXP cross-references, and the wins.json schema's tldr/detail/citations relationships in ways that no rewriter-side audit can validate without analyst judgment. (2) The operator's content-dense exclusion of WIN surfaces is a deliberate editorial discipline; building F as WIN-eligible would create a back-door around that discipline. (3) EXP-401 demonstrated that WIN compression IS qualitatively different from mechanical rewriting — load-bearing-argument identification is editorial judgment, not category-tag-driven prose restructure. F-on-WIN would invert that division of labor.

C5 (HTML tag balance) — same bypass logic.

RWR_rubric_additions_for_F.RWR_10_natural_argument_boundaries: Applies to: F rewrites only. Test: for each entry in proposed_block_boundaries, locate the starts_at_excerpt in original_text. Examine the 2-3 sentences before and after the boundary point. Does the boundary sit at a topic shift, logical-pivot, or argument-completion point? Fail modes: Major if a boundary slices a load-bearing logical structure (e.g., between an IF clause and its THEN, or between a methodology setup and the conclusion that depends on it). Moderate if a boundary sits at a paragraph break but the paragraphs share a single argument's exposition. Common false positives: don't penalize boundaries that produce mildly-uneven sub-block lengths — the goal is argument coherence, not byte-balance.
RWR_rubric_additions_for_F.RWR_11_independent_scoreable_plausibilit: Applies to: F rewrites only. Test (three parts): (a) Read each proposed sub-block's expected content (from original_text between consecutive starts_at_excerpt boundaries). Confirm every paragraph from original_text is allocated to exactly one sub-block — no orphans, no duplicates. (b) Confirm sub-block order in proposed_block_boundaries matches the paragraph order in original_text. F is not allowed to reorder (that's Category D's job, on a single block). (c) For each sub-block, spot-check the rewriter's independent_scoreable prediction. Is the projected sub-block-length plausibly within target_max (or within a content-dense multiplier of it)? If the rewriter predicts independent_scoreable=true for a sub-block that would still be 2x+ target_max, flag as minor (calibration signal). Fail modes: Major on any loss/duplicate paragraph allocation, or any reorder. Moderate on systematic over-optimism in independent_scoreable predictions (>2 sub-blocks predicted scoreable that are clearly still too long).

RW_schema_new_fields: proposed_block_boundaries (array, present iff rewrite_category_tags includes 'F') — array of objects per F-Q2 above
rewritten_text MAY be null when rewrite_category_tags === ['F'] (otherwise required)
schema_field_extensions_for_F.score_record_new_field: status (optional, default null; values: null | 'surface-no-longer-exists'). Set by sloppytoppy-score when surface enumeration drops the ID.
schema_field_extensions_for_F.RW_state_machine_new_status: 'editorial-split-pending-operator'. Reachable from 'approved' for F-RWs only. Settled to 'integrated' manually by operator after the editorial-split commit lands; settled to 'superseded' if operator decides not to apply the split.

decider_intake_changes_for_F.step_1m_B_F_branch: Within Step 1m.B (drain approved RWs), add an early check: if RW.rewrite_category_tags includes 'F', do NOT run find/replace — instead, set RW.status='editorial-split-pending-operator' and write an HNOTE-OPERATOR-F-PROPOSAL-<NNN> with the proposed_block_boundaries + the RW's summary_for_decider (carried over from curmudgeon's review.summary_for_decider). Commit and skip the rest of Step 1m.B for this RW. The HNOTE follows the dual-write rule (write to monitor/decisions/human-notes.json AND monitor/analyst/human-notes.json so all relevant agents see it on next run).
decider_intake_changes_for_F.rationale: Decider's find/replace + test.js + commit path is for inline content edits. F is structural — the work product is a proposed plan, not a replacement text. Splitting this into a dedicated decider branch + an operator HNOTE keeps the contract clean: decider does not auto-apply structural changes.

rewriter_prompt_changes_for_F.step_4c_categorize_F_added: Append F to the category list with the description: 'F (editorial section-split, propose-only)': section_details_block surface where target_max is exceeded by >2.5x AND Categories A-E cannot bring composite above acceptable_floor. Fix: propose 4-6 sub-block boundaries at natural argument breaks, writing proposed_block_boundaries[] in the RW. rewritten_text is null. Operator + cowork-claude apply the editorial split. NOT WIN-eligible — WIN compression goes through analyst-commissioned mode.
rewriter_prompt_changes_for_F.step_4d_drafting_F_branch: When F is chosen: identify topic shifts in original_text (paragraph-level reading). Look for transitions like new section-of-the-argument intro ('Now we turn to...', 'Having established X, consider Y'), methodology shifts ('The above used the dashboard convention; we now switch to the V51 convention'), or claim-completion points (a final paragraph wrapping up an argument before the next argument starts). Propose 4-6 boundaries spaced to keep each sub-block under 2x target_max ideally. Excerpts must be verbatim from original_text (audit-script C12 validates).
rewriter_prompt_changes_for_F.step_4f_gate_F_special_case: F's predicted-delta is computed per sub-block: predicted_composite_after for each sub-block ≥ acceptable_floor (7.5). Aggregate min(predicted_composite_after across sub-blocks) is the F-RW's predicted_composite_after for delta-gate purposes (the LOWEST sub-block must clear floor). If even the worst sub-block clears floor, F is the right move. If multiple sub-blocks would still be below floor after split, F is the WRONG move — write a PUNT recommending content-dense reclassification or analyst-commissioned restructure instead.

G_Q1_mechanical_move.question: What's the rewriter's mechanical move? What's the prompt-level instruction for finding the preview content?
G_Q1_mechanical_move.answer: Three-step move. (1) Detect forward references in original_text matching the patterns: 'See Section <X.Y>', 'see Section <X.Y>', 'See Part <N>', 'see Part <N>', 'See WIN-<NNN>', 'see WIN-<NNN>', 'See ISS-<NNN>', 'see ISS-<NNN>', 'See PRED-<NNN>', 'see PRED-<NNN>'. (2) For each reference, resolve the target in the rewriter's clone: Section X.Y → sections.json lookup for matching <details id> attribute → read the <summary> text; Part N → sections.json file part<N>.html → read the page-title or first <h2>; WIN-NNN → wins.json[id=WIN-NNN] → read claim_tldr or detail_claim; ISS-NNN → monitor/decisions/open-issues.json or closed-issues.json → read title; PRED-NNN → predictions.json → read claim_tldr. (3) Synthesize a 1-sentence preview that paraphrases the target's summary/TLDR/title using load-bearing nouns from the target. Replace 'See Section 1.8' with 'Section 1.8 [paraphrase of 1.8 summary].'
G_Q1_mechanical_move.preference_order_when_multiple_sources_e: claim_tldr (WIN) > <summary> text (section) > section heading > raw title. Always prefer the most concise authoritative source the target carries.
G_Q1_mechanical_move.what_counts_as_a_paraphrase: A paraphrase preserves the target's load-bearing nouns and the directional claim. 'Section 1.8 closes the refraction-fix escape' paraphrases Section 1.8's summary 'Refraction cannot reinterpret H(r) as apparent — Polaris calibration blocks the rescue.' New framing (e.g., calling Section 1.8 'the refraction debunk' when the summary calls it 'closes the refraction-fix escape') is RWR-5 territory.

G_Q2_RWR_5_interaction.question: How does G interact with RWR-5 ('no new claims')? Is the preview a paraphrase or a new framing?
G_Q2_RWR_5_interaction.answer: G's preview MUST be a paraphrase of the resolved target's existing content. The preview is rooted in the target's claim_tldr/summary/title — text that already exists in our review. Paraphrasing existing content is NOT a new claim. New framing IS a new claim and rejected. Explicit RWR-5 carve-out language added: 'Exception for Category G: a 1-sentence paraphrase of a resolved forward-reference target's claim_tldr or <summary> is NOT a new claim, provided the paraphrase preserves the target's load-bearing nouns and directional claim.' The carve-out is narrowly scoped — applies only to G-tagged RWs where the rewriter has populated a new preview_source_ref field (see schema additions below) documenting which surface the paraphrase came from.

G_Q3_token_cost.question: Does G require reading multiple files (wins.json + sections.json)? Token-cost increase?
G_Q3_token_cost.answer: Yes, G requires additional reads. Typical cost-impact estimate: a section_details_block surface with 10 forward references (e.g., the PUNT-002 surface part6.html#p6-610-9) requires resolving 10 targets. The targets are typically already loaded for other reasons (the rewriter reads wins.json and sections.json wholesale during Step 1; per-reference resolution is a JSON lookup, not a fresh file read). Token cost: ~50-150 tokens per resolved reference (the target's summary/TLDR is short). For a 10-reference surface, ~500-1500 tokens. For a 1-3-reference surface (the common case), <500 tokens. Acceptable per the PROP-041 cost_analysis budget (~20-40k input per run). The bigger cost is on the OUTPUT side: G expands a 'See Section 1.8' (4 words) to a 1-sentence preview (~20 words). For a 10-reference surface, the rewritten_text grows by ~150 words. This could push some surfaces above the target_max length penalty even after Category A/B sentence-splits. Mitigation: rewriter's predict-delta computation (Step 4e) accounts for G's word-add; if the surface tips over after G application, the rewriter can drop the lowest-priority reference back to bare-reference form (judgment call documented in the per-tag rationale).
G_Q3_token_cost.summary: Token cost is acceptable. Output-length cost is the real consideration; predict-delta math handles it.

G_Q4_failure_modes_drift.question: Failure mode when a forward reference points to content that doesn't exist or has been deleted (drift)?
G_Q4_failure_modes_drift.answer: Three drift modes. (1) Reference points to a section_id/win_id/pred_id that doesn't exist in the rewriter's clone → log to per-tag rationale ('forward-reference target Section X.Y not found in current sections.json — likely stale reference'), skip THAT reference in the rewrite (leave bare 'See Section X.Y' in place), and continue with G on the other references. Do NOT punt the whole rewrite — partial G is fine. Issue an analyst-attention HNOTE (via the dual-write rule) flagging the broken reference for analyst follow-up. (2) Reference points to a target that exists but whose summary/TLDR is empty → use the section heading or title as fallback; if no usable preview source exists, skip the reference (same handling as case 1). (3) Reference points to a target that has been substantively rewritten since the original surface's authorship → the rewriter has no way to detect this (no history-aware diffing in scope). Accept the rewriter will paraphrase the CURRENT target, which is the correct semantics (the preview reflects what the reader will find when they follow the link).

rewriter_prompt_changes_for_G.step_4e_predict_delta_G_branch: For each G substitution, forward_references subtraction goes from -1 (per reference) toward 0. Length increases by ~16 words per substitution (the preview expansion). Subtract the length-increase impact from the projected length-axis score (per rubric §3 length curve). Final composite delta = +0.6 * forward_references_recovery - 0.4 * length_decay.

task_3_category_H_full_spec.title: Category H — outbound link-out preview (narrowed per addendum)
task_3_category_H_full_spec.addendum_scope_narrowing_applied: Per 2026-05-16 EXP-401 compression-experiment addendum: H is NOT a depth-content-replacement category. H is strictly a mechanical link-preview move. Real content compression (3-5x reduction via editorial judgment about load-bearing arguments) is analyst-commissioned work, NOT rewriter work. This narrowing is applied throughout the spec below.

H_Q1_trigger.question: What's the trigger? Only fires when surface is over X×target_max? Or any surface where the rewriter judges depth content can be replaced with a summary+link?
H_Q1_trigger.answer: TRIGGER NARROWED per addendum. H fires ONLY when original_text contains specific bare outbound references where the link target is NOT auto-readable from the rewriter's clone — meaning: (a) `<a href="https://...">` to external URLs (papers, datasets, news sites), (b) `<a href="/research/...">` or other intra-site links to pages outside wins.json/sections.json scope, (c) 'see <document.pdf>' / 'see <external-author><year>' inline references that the rewriter cannot resolve internally. H does NOT fire on surface-length-only signals. H does NOT replace whole paragraphs with link-outs. H is a per-reference mechanical preview, same shape as G but for unreadable targets.
H_Q1_trigger.explicit_non_triggers: H does NOT fire when: (1) the surface is just long but contains no outbound references (that's F or analyst-compression territory); (2) the surface contains internal references resolvable from the clone (that's G); (3) the rewriter could in principle navigate to a target via web-fetch (rewriter has no web-fetch tool by design — local clone only).

H_Q2_link_destination.question: What's the link destination? Existing dome-review pages? External research? New dedicated page?
H_Q2_link_destination.answer: H DOES NOT CREATE OR CHANGE link destinations. H operates on existing link destinations that are ALREADY in original_text. The link's href value passes through verbatim. H only modifies the human-readable text surrounding/preceding the link to add a 1-sentence preview of what the reader will find at the link. Creating a NEW link destination (linking to a page that didn't exist before) is OUT OF SCOPE for H — that's editorial content-architecture territory and goes through operator-led changes.

H_Q3_content_preservation_discipline.question: Content-preservation discipline? Does the audit script need to follow links?
H_Q3_content_preservation_discipline.answer: NO link-following by the audit script. Under the narrow H scope, the rewriter does NOT extract content from external pages — H is a preview based on anchor text + surrounding context only. Content-preservation discipline is therefore SIMPLE: numbers_preserved and citations_preserved both apply normally (no numbers or citations are removed — they remain in the surface, just with an added preview clause). The audit script's C1/C2 checks run normally on H-tagged RWs.

H_Q4_D_C_alignment.question: How does this interact with D-C operator policy? Is H the operational mechanism for D-C?
H_Q4_D_C_alignment.answer: NO. H is NOT the operational mechanism for D-C per addendum. D-C says WIN surfaces should NEVER expand inline; long WIN debunks should link out to underlying research. The operational mechanism for D-C is the analyst-commissioned content-compression mode (HNOTE → analyst → EXP → decider integrates, exactly as EXP-401 demonstrated). H stays as the narrow mechanical link-preview category; it does NOT carry the D-C policy weight.
H_Q4_D_C_alignment.implication_for_phasing: Because H is no longer the D-C mechanism, the operator does NOT need to wait for H to ship before the D-C policy is operational. D-C is operational TODAY via existing analyst-EXP plumbing (no new agent or category needed). This significantly reduces the urgency of H delivery.

H_Q5_fragmentation_risk_and_counter_argu.question: Inverse risk? Linking out too aggressively could fragment the argument.
H_Q5_fragmentation_risk_and_counter_argu.answer: Under the narrow H scope, fragmentation risk is MINIMAL. H only adds preview sentences to existing outbound links — it does NOT relocate content to other pages, does NOT create new pages, does NOT decide that a paragraph 'belongs elsewhere.' The reader's experience: they encounter the same outbound links they would have encountered without H, plus a 1-sentence preview telling them what they'll find if they click through. That is a strict improvement on the no-preview baseline (the reader can decide whether the link is worth following without clicking).
H_Q5_fragmentation_risk_and_counter_argu.counter_argument_section_below: See 'counter_argument_section' below for the steelman of WHY one might still object.

patches.old_string:
Specific patterns to avoid:
- **Don't paraphrase load-bearing nouns.** "Tesla 11.79 Hz prediction" stays — don't render as "Tesla's resonance claim" (RWR-2 hole).
- **Don't drop hedges.** "approximately", "on the order of", "~2%" all stay as written.

patches.new_string:
Specific patterns to avoid:
- **Don't paraphrase load-bearing nouns.** "Tesla 11.79 Hz prediction" stays — don't render as "Tesla's resonance claim" (RWR-2 hole).
- **Don't drop hedges.** "approximately", "on the order of", "~2%" all stay as written.
- **Don't add citations.** RWR-5 rejects new claims. Only restructure what's there.
- **Don't move conclusions before premises** if the premise is uniquely needed first (RWR-4).
- **G/H discipline (PROP-041 amendment-002):** previews paraphrase EXISTING content. For G, the source is the resolved target's claim_tldr/summary/title — the load-bearing nouns must match. For H, the source is the link's anchor + 1-2 surrounding sentences — do not synthesize claims about external content. New framing fails RWR-12 (G) and RWR-13 (H).

**Category G drafting (when chosen):**
1. Enumerate bare forward-references in original_text matching: 'See Section <X.Y>', 'see Section <X.Y>', 'See Part <N>', 'See WIN-<NNN>', 'See ISS-<NNN>', 'See PRED-<NNN>' (case-insensitive on 'see').
2. For each, resolve target in clone:
   - Section X.Y → sections.json → `<details id="...">` → read `<summary>` text
   - Part N → sections.json file part<N>.html → read page-title/first `<h2>`
   - WIN-NNN → wins.json[id=WIN-NNN] → read claim_tldr (preferred) or detail_claim
   - ISS-NNN → open-issues.json / closed-issues.json → read title
   - PRED-NNN → predictions.json → read claim_tldr
3. Preference order when multiple sources exist: claim_tldr > `<summary>` > heading > title.
4. Synthesize 1-sentence preview using load-bearing nouns from the resolved source. Replace bare reference with: '<target-name> <preview>.'
5. Populate `preview_source_refs[]` entry per schema.
6. On resolution failure: skip THAT reference (leave bare), log to per-tag rationale, file analyst-attention HNOTE for the broken reference.

**Category H drafting (when chosen):**
1. Enumerate outbound references in original_text: `<a href="http://...">` / `<a href="https://...">` / `<a href="/research/...">` / inline 'see <doc.pdf>' / inline 'see <external-author><year>'.
2. For each, extract anchor text + 1-2 sentences of surrounding context.
3. Synthesize 1-sentence preview grounded ONLY in anchor + context. Do NOT claim things about the external target the surface doesn't already say.
4. Insert preview adjacent to the link in rewritten_text (before or after, whichever reads more naturally).
5. Populate `link_preview_refs[]` entry per schema.
6. On thin anchor + context: skip THAT reference (cannot ground preview), log to per-tag rationale.

patches.new_string:
**Fail mode:** Minor. Predicted-delta drift is a calibration signal, not a content failure — the rewrite may still be a good rewrite. The recalibration audit (Q-OP-7) catches systematic drift; this check catches per-RW drift.

### RWR-12: G preview is paraphrase, not new framing (PROP-041 amendment-002)

**Applies to:** G rewrites only.

**Test:** For each G-substituted preview in rewritten_text, locate the source via `preview_source_refs`. Read the source's claim_tldr/summary. Confirm the preview's load-bearing nouns appear in the source.

**Fail modes:** Major if the preview introduces a noun (or directional claim) that doesn't appear in the source. Moderate if the preview is technically a paraphrase but uses noticeably heavier/lighter hedging than the source. Minor on awkward phrasing.

**Common false positives:** Don't penalize tonal smoothing that preserves load-bearing nouns. 'closes the refraction-fix escape' vs 'rules out the refraction reinterpretation' — both preserve the load-bearing 'refraction' noun and the same directional claim; both pass.

**Common true positives:** New framings to flag — preview adds a generalization the source didn't claim ('Section 1.8 establishes that ALL escape attempts fail' when 1.8 only addresses refraction); preview drops a hedge the source carried ('refraction cannot reinterpret' becoming 'refraction does not reinterpret'); preview re-attributes a claim ('Smith showed' when the source attributed it to Jones).

### RWR-13: H preview grounded in anchor or context (PROP-041 amendment-002)

**Applies to:** H rewrites only.

**Test:** For each H-preview, confirm the preview's content is grounded in the link's anchor text OR the 1-2 sentences of context surrounding the link in original_text. The rewriter MUST NOT introduce new claims about the external target's content (e.g., claiming a linked paper 'definitively proves X' when neither the anchor nor the surface's own context says so).

**Fail modes:** Major if the preview makes a claim about external content not supported by anchor/context. Moderate if grounded but with heavier/lighter hedging than the context suggests. Minor on tonal mismatches.

**Interaction with RWR-5:** RWR-5 (no new claims) and RWR-13 (grounded preview) are companion checks. RWR-13 narrows RWR-5 for H: the source-of-paraphrase is the surface's own anchor + adjacent context (which is auto-readable), NOT the external target (which is not readable). H is the only category where the rewriter is restricted to a smaller authoritative-source scope than the full surface.

## Optional free-text field

patches.new_string:
**Category H drafting (when chosen):**
1. Enumerate outbound references in original_text: `<a href="http://...">` / `<a href="https://...">` / `<a href="/research/...">` / inline 'see <doc.pdf>' / inline 'see <external-author><year>'.
2. For each, extract anchor text + 1-2 sentences of surrounding context.
3. Synthesize 1-sentence preview grounded ONLY in anchor + context. Do NOT claim things about the external target the surface doesn't already say.
4. Insert preview adjacent to the link in rewritten_text (before or after, whichever reads more naturally).
5. Populate `link_preview_refs[]` entry per schema.
6. On thin anchor + context: skip THAT reference (cannot ground preview), log to per-tag rationale.

**Category F drafting (when chosen — Phase 2.1):**
1. Confirm trigger criteria: surface_type === 'section_details_block' AND length-axis is below 1.0 (target_max exceeded by >2.5×) AND no Category A-E-G-H combination clears acceptable_floor when applied alone.
2. Identify topic shifts in original_text (paragraph-level reading). Patterns:
   - new-section-of-argument intros: 'Now we turn to...', 'Having established X, consider Y'
   - methodology shifts: 'The above used the dashboard convention; we now switch to the V51 convention'
   - claim-completion points: final paragraph wrapping up an argument before the next argument starts
3. Propose 4-6 boundaries spaced to keep each sub-block under 2× target_max ideally. Boundaries MUST sit at paragraph breaks, NEVER mid-sentence.
4. For each boundary, build a `proposed_block_boundaries[]` entry: split_index (0-indexed), proposed_subblock_id (suggestion like '<parent-id>a', '<parent-id>b'), proposed_subblock_title (short editorial title like '4.1.1 Ruler definition'), starts_at_excerpt (~30 verbatim words from original_text marking the boundary), rationale (1 sentence explaining why this is a natural break), independent_scoreable (boolean prediction).
5. Set rewritten_text to null. F is structural — no inline rewrite.
6. Predict-delta: per F-Q4, F's predicted_composite_after is the MIN composite across all proposed sub-blocks. If even the worst sub-block clears acceptable_floor, F is the right move. If multiple sub-blocks remain below floor, F is WRONG — write a PUNT instead recommending content-dense reclassification or analyst-commissioned restructure.

### RWR-10: F proposed boundaries at natural argument breaks (PROP-041 amendment-002 Phase 2.1)

**Applies to:** F rewrites only.

**Test:** For each entry in `proposed_block_boundaries`, locate the `starts_at_excerpt` in original_text. Examine the 2-3 sentences before and after the boundary point. Does the boundary sit at a topic shift, logical-pivot, or argument-completion point?

**Fail modes:** Major if a boundary slices a load-bearing logical structure (e.g., between an IF clause and its THEN, or between a methodology setup and the conclusion that depends on it). Moderate if a boundary sits at a paragraph break but the paragraphs share a single argument's exposition. Minor on systematic over-tight boundary spacing that creates lopsided sub-blocks without a content rationale.

**Common false positives:** Don't penalize boundaries that produce mildly-uneven sub-block lengths — argument coherence trumps byte-balance. Don't penalize for boundary at a topic-shift the curmudgeon would have chosen slightly differently — subjective placement within the same natural-break region is fine.

### RWR-11: F independent_scoreable plausibility, no loss/reorder (PROP-041 amendment-002 Phase 2.1)

**Applies to:** F rewrites only.

**Test (three parts):** (a) Read each proposed sub-block's expected content (paragraphs between consecutive `starts_at_excerpt` boundaries). Confirm every paragraph from original_text is allocated to exactly one sub-block — no orphans, no duplicates. (b) Confirm sub-block order in `proposed_block_boundaries` matches paragraph order in original_text. F does NOT reorder — that's Category D's job on a single block. (c) Spot-check the rewriter's `independent_scoreable` prediction per sub-block. Is the projected sub-block length plausibly within target_max (or within a content-dense multiplier of it)? If the rewriter predicts independent_scoreable=true for a sub-block clearly still 2× target_max, flag as calibration signal.

**Fail modes:** Major on any loss/duplicate paragraph allocation, or any reorder. Moderate on systematic over-optimism in independent_scoreable predictions (>2 sub-blocks predicted scoreable that remain clearly too long). Minor on small projected-length miscalls.

### RWR-12: G preview is paraphrase, not new framing (PROP-041 amendment-002)

task_6_counter_argument_section_mandator.title: Counter-arguments: the case for NOT adding F/G/H
task_6_counter_argument_section_mandator.purpose: Per operator preference, steelman the case against each new category. This section is mandatory by directive.

counters.framed_as: F is an editorial decision, not a mechanical rewrite. Pushing it into the rewriter agent inverts the operator-as-content-architect model. F should be human-decision, not Opus-decision.
counters.steelman: Argument-boundary selection IS judgment. Where to split a 4178-word section block depends on the argument's load-bearing structure, the operator's editorial taste about what counts as a self-contained sub-argument, and tacit knowledge about how the dome model's defenders frame their counter-arguments. A category-driven mechanical agent applying pattern-match-on-paragraph-shape rules will produce splits that look natural to the rule but cut load-bearing IF/THEN chains the operator would have preserved. The first F-RW will need significant operator revision; by the third, the rewriter will have internalized the operator's split-preferences via repeated feedback — but that's a months-long fine-tuning loop that creates churn without clear net benefit. Better: operator does the F work directly (5 sections, each 30 min editorial review + apply = ~2.5h total), one-shot. The PROP-041 plumbing for F's RW shape is over-engineering for a job the operator can do faster than the rewriter can propose.
counters.rebuttal: F is propose-only, not auto-apply. The rewriter SUGGESTS boundaries; curmudgeon validates against RWR-10/11; operator + cowork-claude apply. Same propose-only-with-curmudgeon-validation model as A-E and G/H. The cost is the rewriter's per-surface analysis time + curmudgeon's review time; the benefit is operator gets a structured proposal with rationale instead of starting from scratch. For 5 high-priority section_details_block surfaces, the rewriter's analysis is faster than the operator's first-pass read. Operator time is reduced from 'propose + apply' to 'review proposal + apply.' Net win even if rewriter's proposed boundaries need editorial revision before apply.
counters.remaining_concerns: If RWR-10 rejection rate is high (>40% of F-RWs rejected on first attempt), the proposal-to-edit pipeline is creating work, not saving it. Phased rollout (Phase 2.1 deferred until G+H land and prove out the workflow) and the operator-supervised first-run discipline (per PROP-041 phased_implementation_plan) mitigate this. If after 5 F-RWs the rejection rate exceeds 40%, deprecate F and route to analyst-commissioned restructure.

counters.framed_as: G requires the rewriter to read the referenced section, which is a token-cost increase that could double the per-surface cost. Worth it?
counters.steelman: PROP-041's cost analysis estimated ~20-40k input tokens per run. G's resolution step requires reading wins.json (~150KB, already loaded for surface enumeration) and sections.json (~300KB, also already loaded). The per-reference resolution is a JSON lookup, not a fresh file read — token cost is in the SYNTHESIS step (writing the preview), not the LOOKUP step. Synthesis cost: ~50-150 output tokens per substitution. For a 10-reference surface (PUNT-002's profile), that's 500-1500 output tokens added per surface. Across a 5-surface batch, that's 2500-7500 extra output tokens. PROP-041 budget was 15-30k output per run. Adding 2500-7500 brings us to 17500-37500. Still within bound, but the margin tightens. If the rewriter encounters a content-dense run where every surface has many forward-references, the upper bound could push over budget. Counter-counter: cost analysis budgeted for typical surfaces, not the worst case. The first 21 high-priority surfaces in the eligible queue have an average of ~4 forward-references each (per PUNT-002 + Step 1 reading of scores.json subtractions); ~20 output tokens × 4 substitutions × 5 surfaces = 400 tokens extra per run. Negligible against the 15-30k budget.
counters.rebuttal: The forward_references subtraction is the second-largest understandability subtractor across all below-floor surfaces in the current scores.json — 42 of 168 below-floor surfaces have forward_references > -3 in their subtraction profile (25% prevalence per first-run analysis). Fixing this for 25% of the eligible queue at the cost of <1k extra output tokens per run is a clean win. The first-run PUNT-002 explicitly called out forward-references as a category gap.
counters.remaining_concerns: If G's predict-delta calibration is wrong (e.g., the rewriter consistently overestimates forward_references recovery), the Q-OP-7 recalibration audit catches it within 5 integrated rewrites. Acceptable.

counters.framed_as: H risks fragmenting the review — readers get a teaser + a link, click through, lose context. The current inline-everything design is a deliberate choice for readability.
counters.steelman: The dome review's existing design favors inline-everything: WIN debunks are detailed and self-contained; readers don't need to click through to verify a claim. This is a deliberate accessibility choice (some readers don't have reliable internet, some are on mobile with limited bandwidth, some are reading printed PDF exports). H breaks this design by encouraging the rewriter to insert previews that imply 'click here for more.' Even with the narrowed scope (preview-only, not content-replacement), H signals to readers that the surface is incomplete — go elsewhere for the full picture. That's a UX regression. The first-run analysis showed forward_references firing on 25% of below-floor surfaces; H's outbound-link equivalent will fire on FAR fewer (most surfaces have 0-1 outbound links per § rough estimate). For modest reach, H buys a UX downgrade.
counters.rebuttal: Per addendum scope-narrowing, H is for surfaces that ALREADY have outbound references — H doesn't introduce new links, it just adds preview sentences to existing ones. The 'inline-everything' design is unchanged; H only enhances the few links that exist. Counter-counter: but in EXISTING inline-everything surfaces, outbound links are rare and often decorative (e.g., 'see [original publication]' as a citation flourish, not a navigational invitation). Adding a 1-sentence preview to a decorative link might make the link feel more navigational than it should. Counter-counter-counter: the rewriter's Step 4d.H.3 discipline ('synthesize preview grounded ONLY in anchor + context, do NOT claim things about the external target the surface doesn't already say') prevents the preview from oversell. The preview describes what the reader will find based on what the surface already says about the link — that's a transparent improvement, not a navigational redirect.
counters.remaining_concerns: Per addendum, H is no longer the operational mechanism for D-C. The pressure to ship H quickly is lower. If the operator decides H is too small a payoff for the design-discipline trade-off, it can be dropped without affecting G or F. Recommend: ship H with G in Phase 2 if both look clean; defer H if the first audit-script + rubric work surfaces issues.

counters.framed_as: Three new categories is a lot of new surface area for a rewriter that's run exactly once and produced exactly one approved-by-audit RW. We're scaling the design before we know the design works.
counters.steelman: First-run produced 1 RW + 4 PUNTs. We don't yet know whether the curmudgeon-on-rewrite checklist (RWR-1..9) actually catches what we want it to catch. We don't yet know whether the audit-script catches the failures we expect. We don't yet know whether predict-delta calibration is in the ballpark. Adding F/G/H before these baseline questions are answered means we'll be debugging multiple new categories simultaneously when problems surface, with no clean A/B comparison against a stable A-E baseline.
counters.rebuttal: The PUNT records are the strongest signal we have about category gaps. PUNT-001 explicitly described 'Category F'; PUNT-002 explicitly described 'Category G' (under that name, with the exact mechanism). The category gaps are not speculative — the first run identified them empirically. Deferring F/G/H means deferring fixes the rewriter has already requested. Compromise: Phase 2 lands G+H (lowest architectural risk, addresses 25% prevalence subtractor). Phase 2.1 lands F (highest architectural risk, single dominant defect class). The phasing matches risk gradient.
counters.remaining_concerns: If RW-001 is rejected by curmudgeon on substantive grounds (load-bearing connective dropped, etc.), the lesson informs the F/G/H rubric BEFORE those land. Operator may want to delay amendment-002 application until at least one A-E RW has gone through the full intake → curmudgeon → decider → integrated loop. That's a reasonable hold; this amendment lands as 'implementation-pending-operator-review' so the timing is operator's choice.

open_questions_for_operator.question: Phase 2 vs Phase 2.1 split: ship G+H first and defer F, or land all three together?
open_questions_for_operator.tinker_recommendation: Phased. G+H first (lower risk, clear payoff for forward_references subtractor at 25% prevalence). F second after operator validates the G/H workflow.

open_questions_for_operator.question: F's editorial-split-pending-operator status: should operator-decline (status='superseded') be one-way, or can the operator re-open it later if circumstances change?
open_questions_for_operator.tinker_recommendation: One-way. The original F-RW captures the rewriter's proposal at draft time; if the surface evolves, a new F-RW should be drafted (cooldown 30d). Re-opening superseded RWs creates audit-trail ambiguity.

open_questions_for_operator.question: G's preview-source preference order (claim_tldr > <summary> > heading > title) — is this the right priority for the dome-review domain?
open_questions_for_operator.tinker_recommendation: Yes — claim_tldr is the most concise authoritative form when WINs are involved; section <summary> tags are the natural preview for sections. Operator can revise if early G-RWs surface a preference mismatch.

open_questions_for_operator.question: H's narrowing per addendum drops the D-C operational-mechanism role. Should we explicitly document the analyst-commissioned content-compression mode as the D-C mechanism (file under DIRECTIVE-20260516-005 follow-up or a separate PROP)?

Compression-mode is enough of a design surface (HNOTE-driven analyst mode, EXP authorship pattern, decider integration) that bundling it into this amendment overloads the scope. File a PROP-042-analyst-compression-mode as the home for that design work.

open_questions_for_operator.question: DIRECTIVE-20260516-006 (analyst HNOTE priority bump) is a sibling open directive. Should this amendment's HNOTE-OPERATOR-F-PROPOSAL flow be designed to integrate with the priority-bump fix?

footer.summary: Single comprehensive amendment adding F (editorial section-split, propose-only, Phase 2.1), G (internal forward-reference preview, Phase 2), and H (outbound link-out preview, narrowed scope, Phase 2). 17 phased patches across 5 files. F.7-style audit-script bypass for F-only RWs; RWR-10/11/12/13 rubric additions; new schema fields for preview_source_refs / link_preview_refs / proposed_block_boundaries; new RW state 'editorial-split-pending-operator' for the F integration loop. Regression-checked clean against PROP-041 amendment-001, PROP-037/038/040, and existing append-only / NEVER_PUSH disciplines. Counter-argument section steelmans the case for NOT adding each category, with rebuttals and remaining-concern bounds. Tinker recommends Phase 2 (G+H) lands first, Phase 2.1 (F) after operator validates the G/H workflow.
